text: ‘Enough for thee that thou shouldst comfort him’; the meaning of the Septuagint Greek is, ‘And be comforted for his loss’.
Literally, ‘bowing down his strength before his feet’, but the Greek has, ‘its strength’.
Some would interpret the first part of this verse differently, taking it with verse 36 and giving it the sense ‘there will be no living or going to and fro in common’.
The last fourteen words of the chapter really belong, as the Greek shews, to the beginning of the next chapter. As applied to the manual labourers described above, they give exactly the wrong sense.
